sentences.chinese: 廣辭曰：「臣部為前將軍，今大將軍乃徙臣出東道，且臣結髮而與匈奴戰，乃令一得當單于，臣願居前，先死單于。」
sentences.literal: Guang declined, saying, "My division was made vanguard general; now the supreme commander has shifted me out by the eastern road. Moreover, since I bound up my hair I have fought the Xiongnu; now when I might at last match the Chanyu, I wish to take the van and die confronting the Chanyu."
sentences.idiomatic: Li Guang protested: "I was named vanguard, yet the commander-in-chief has shunted me onto the eastern track. I have fought the Xiongnu since I was a young man; now that I might finally meet the Chanyu in battle, I ask to lead the first line and strike him head-on."

sentences.chinese: 大將軍陰受上指，以為李廣數奇，毋令當單于，恐不得所欲。
sentences.literal: The supreme commander secretly received the emperor's pointer, thinking Li Guang's fortune repeatedly odd, not ordering him to face the Chanyu, fearing he would not obtain what was desired.
sentences.idiomatic: Wei Qing had private orders from the throne: Li Guang's luck was thought ill-starred, so he must not be allowed to bear the brunt against the Chanyu lest the campaign miscarry.

sentences.chinese: 是時，公孫敖新失侯，為中將軍，大將軍亦欲使敖與俱當單于，故徙廣。
sentences.literal: At this time Gongsun Ao had newly lost his marquisate and was central general; the supreme commander also wished to make Ao together with him face the Chanyu, therefore shifted Guang.
sentences.idiomatic: Gongsun Ao had just been stripped of his fief and was serving as central general; Wei Qing wanted him at his side for the strike on the Chanyu, which is why Li Guang was reassigned.

sentences.chinese: 廣知之，固辭。
sentences.literal: Guang knew it and firmly declined.
sentences.idiomatic: Li Guang saw through the arrangement and refused again and again.

sentences.chinese: 大將軍弗聽，令長史封書與廣之莫府，曰：「急詣部，如書。」
sentences.literal: The supreme commander did not listen; he ordered the chief clerk to seal a letter and deliver it to Guang's headquarters, saying, "Hasten to your unit according to the letter."
sentences.idiomatic: Wei Qing would not hear him out and sent his chief clerk with sealed orders to Li Guang's field office: "Report to your command at once—no argument."

sentences.chinese: 廣不謝大將軍而起行，意象慍怒而就部，引兵與右將軍食其合軍出東道。
sentences.literal: Guang did not thank the supreme commander but rose and went; his bearing seemed resentful and angry as he went to his unit; he led troops to join the army of General of the Right Shiqi and combined armies out the eastern road.
sentences.idiomatic: Li Guang left without a word of thanks, his face dark with anger, took his post, and marched to join General of the Right Zhao Shiqi for the eastern approach.

sentences.chinese: 惑失道，後大將軍。
sentences.literal: Confused, they lost the way and lagged behind the supreme commander.
sentences.idiomatic: They wandered off the route and arrived too late to support Wei Qing's main blow.

sentences.chinese: 大將軍與單于接戰，單于遁走，弗能得而還。
sentences.literal: The supreme commander joined battle with the Chanyu; the Chanyu fled; they could not catch him and returned.
sentences.idiomatic: Wei Qing engaged the Chanyu, who broke off and escaped; the Han pursuit could not run him down.

sentences.chinese: 南絕幕，乃遇兩將軍。
sentences.literal: Going south they crossed the desert; then they met the two generals.
sentences.idiomatic: On the march south across the Gobi they at last met Li Guang's and Zhao Shiqi's columns.

sentences.chinese: 廣已見大將軍，還入軍。
sentences.literal: Guang had already seen the supreme commander and returned into his army.
sentences.idiomatic: After a brief interview with Wei Qing, Li Guang withdrew to his own camp.

sentences.chinese: 大將軍使長史持糒醪遺廣，因問廣、食其失道狀，曰：「青欲上書報天子失軍曲折。」
sentences.literal: The supreme commander sent the chief clerk bearing dried rations and ale to present to Guang, and thereupon asked Guang and Shiqi the circumstances of losing the way, saying, "Qing wishes to memorialize the Son of Heaven reporting the twists of the lost-army affair."
sentences.idiomatic: Wei Qing sent his chief clerk with food and wine for Li Guang and questioned him and Zhao Shiqi about getting lost, adding that he meant to report the whole tangled affair to the emperor.

sentences.chinese: 廣未對。
sentences.literal: Guang did not reply.
sentences.idiomatic: Li Guang said nothing.

sentences.chinese: 大將軍長史急責廣之莫府上簿。
sentences.literal: The supreme commander's chief clerk urgently pressed Guang's headquarters to submit the roster.
sentences.idiomatic: The chief clerk then demanded that Li Guang's staff present him for the formal inquiry.

sentences.chinese: 廣曰：「諸校尉亡罪，乃我自失道。
sentences.literal: Guang said, "The various colonels are without guilt; it was I myself who lost the way.
sentences.idiomatic: Li Guang said, "My colonels are blameless—the fault for losing the way is mine alone.

sentences.chinese: 吾今自上簿。」
sentences.literal: I will now go myself to submit the roster."
sentences.idiomatic: I will go myself to make the deposition."

sentences.chinese: 至莫府，謂其麾下曰：「廣結髮與匈奴大小七十餘戰，今幸從大將軍出接單于兵，而大將軍徙廣部行回遠，又迷失道，豈非天哉！
sentences.literal: Reaching headquarters, he said to those beneath his banner, "Since I bound my hair I have fought the Xiongnu in more than seventy engagements, large and small; now, though I had the fortune to follow the supreme commander out against the Chanyu's host, the supreme commander shifted my unit onto a long detour, and I again lost the way—is this not Heaven!
sentences.idiomatic: At his headquarters he told his officers, "Since I first took up arms I have fought the Xiongnu more than seventy times, large and small. I thought I would finally ride with the commander-in-chief against the Chanyu—only to be sent the long way round and lose the trail. Can this be anything but fate?

sentences.chinese: 且廣年六十餘，終不能復對刀筆之吏矣！」
sentences.literal: Moreover Guang is more than sixty; in the end I cannot again face clerks with knife and brush!"
sentences.idiomatic: I am past sixty—I will not end my days answering to some clerk with his brush and knife!"

sentences.chinese: 遂引刀自剄。
sentences.literal: Thereupon he drew a knife and cut his own throat.
sentences.idiomatic: He drew his sword and cut his own throat.

sentences.chinese: 百姓聞之，知與不知，老壯皆為垂泣。
sentences.literal: The hundred surnames hearing it, whether knowing or not knowing, old and young all for him shed tears.
sentences.idiomatic: When word spread, everyone who heard it, whether they had known him or not, wept—old and young alike.

sentences.chinese: 而右將軍獨下吏，當死，贖為遮人。
sentences.literal: But the general of the right alone was handed to the officials; liable to death, he ransomed his sentence and was reduced to commoner status.
sentences.idiomatic: General of the Right Zhao Shiqi alone was handed over to the judges, faced execution, and bought his life back at the price of commoner status.

sentences.chinese: 廣三子，曰當戶、椒、敢，皆為郎。
sentences.literal: Guang had three sons, called Danghu, Jiao, and Gan; all were gentlemen-attendants.
sentences.idiomatic: Li Guang had three sons—Danghu, Jiao, and Gan—each of whom held a post as gentleman-attendant at court.

sentences.chinese: 上與韓嫣戲，嫣少不遜，當戶擊嫣，嫣走，於是上以為能。
sentences.literal: The emperor was sporting with Han Yan; Yan was somewhat insolent; Danghu struck Yan; Yan fled; thereupon the emperor thought him capable.
sentences.idiomatic: Once when the emperor was joking with his favorite Han Yan, the man grew impertinent and Li Danghu struck him; Yan ran off, and the emperor judged the young man spirited.

sentences.chinese: 當戶蚤死，乃拜椒為代郡太守，皆先廣死。
sentences.literal: Danghu died early; then Jiao was appointed governor of Dai commandery; both died before Guang.
sentences.idiomatic: Danghu died young; Jiao was later made governor of Dai. Both predeceased their father.

sentences.chinese: 廣死軍中時，敢從票騎將軍。
sentences.literal: When Guang died in the army, Gan was following the swift-crossing general.
sentences.idiomatic: When Li Guang killed himself on campaign, Li Gan was serving under Huo Qubing.

sentences.chinese: 廣死明年，李蔡以丞相坐詔賜冢地陽陵當得二十畝，蔡盜取三頃，頗賣得四十餘萬，又盜取神道外壖地一畝葬其中，當下獄，自殺。
sentences.literal: The year after Guang died, Li Cai as chancellor was charged because by edict the tomb land granted at Yangling ought to have been twenty mu, yet Cai seized three qing, sold much of it for more than four hundred thousand cash, and also appropriated one mu of land beyond the spirit way for a burial—liable to imprisonment, he killed himself.
sentences.idiomatic: The following year Chancellor Li Cai was charged with embezzling imperial tomb land at Yangling—twenty mu by decree, but he had seized three qing, sold off much of it for over four hundred thousand cash, and appropriated another mu beside the spirit way for a private burial. Facing prison, he took his own life.

sentences.chinese: 敢以校尉從票騎將軍擊胡左賢王，力戰，奪左賢王旗鼓，斬首多，賜爵關內侯，食邑二百戶，代廣為郎中令。
sentences.literal: Gan as colonel followed the swift-crossing general in striking the Hu Worthy King of the Left; he fought hard, seized the Worthy King of the Left's drums and banners, beheaded many, was granted rank as marquis within the passes with two hundred households' food income, and replaced Guang as superintendent of the palace gentlemen.
sentences.idiomatic: Li Gan followed Huo Qubing as a colonel against the Worthy King of the Left, fought fiercely, captured the enemy drums and standards, piled up enough heads to be made marquis within the passes with an income of two hundred households, and succeeded his father as superintendent of gentlemen.

sentences.chinese: 頃之，怨大將軍青之恨其父，乃擊傷大將軍，大將軍匿諱之。
sentences.literal: Before long, resenting that Supreme Commander Qing had wronged his father, he then struck and wounded the supreme commander; the supreme commander concealed and hushed it.
sentences.idiomatic: Soon afterward, nursing a grudge against Wei Qing for his father's death, he assaulted the commander-in-chief and wounded him; Wei Qing kept the matter quiet.

sentences.chinese: 居無何，敢從上雍，至甘泉宮獵，票騎將軍去病怨敢傷青，射殺敢。
sentences.literal: Before long had passed, Gan followed the emperor to Yong; reaching Ganquan Palace to hunt, the swift-crossing general Qu Bing resented that Gan had wounded Qing and shot and killed Gan.
sentences.idiomatic: Not long after, while Li Gan was escorting the emperor on a hunt at Ganquan, Huo Qubing—still furious over the attack on his uncle—shot him dead.

sentences.chinese: 去病時方貴幸，上為諱，云「鹿觸殺之」。
sentences.literal: Qu Bing at the time was just honored and favored; the emperor for him covered it, saying "A deer gored him to death."
sentences.idiomatic: Huo Qubing was then at the height of imperial favor, so the court passed off the killing as a hunting accident—a stag had gored Li Gan.

sentences.chinese: 居歲餘，去病死。
sentences.literal: After more than a year Qu Bing died of illness.
sentences.idiomatic: A little over a year later Huo Qubing himself died of illness.

sentences.chinese: 敢有女為太子中人，愛幸。
sentences.literal: Gan had a daughter who was a palace attendant of the heir apparent and was loved and favored.
sentences.idiomatic: Li Gan left a daughter who served as an attendant in the heir apparent's inner quarters and won the young prince's favor.

sentences.chinese: 敢男禹有寵於太子，然好利，亦有勇。
sentences.literal: Gan's son Yu had favor with the heir apparent, yet loved profit, and also had courage.
sentences.idiomatic: His son Li Yu was a favorite of the heir apparent—bold, but greedy.

sentences.chinese: 嘗與侍中貴人飲，侵陵之，莫敢應。
sentences.literal: Once when drinking with palace-attendant nobles he encroached and bullied them; none dared respond.
sentences.idiomatic: Once at wine with several high-ranking attendants he browbeat them until no one dared answer back.

sentences.chinese: 後訴之上，上召禹，使刺虎，縣下圈中，未至地，有詔引出之。
sentences.literal: Afterward they complained to the emperor; the emperor summoned Yu, ordered him to stab a tiger, suspended him down into the pen; before reaching the ground, there was an edict drawing him out.
sentences.idiomatic: They complained to the throne; the emperor summoned Li Yu for a tiger hunt and had him lowered into the pit on ropes, then countermanded the drop and had him winched up before his feet touched the ground.

sentences.chinese: 禹從落中以劍斫絕累，欲刺虎。
sentences.literal: Yu, while being lowered, with a sword chopped and severed the ropes that bound him, intending to stab the tiger.
sentences.idiomatic: Halfway down he cut himself free with his blade and made as if to fight the animal anyway.

sentences.chinese: 上壯之，遂救止焉。
sentences.literal: The emperor thought him stalwart and thereupon rescued and stopped it.
sentences.idiomatic: The emperor admired his nerve and called off the test.

sentences.chinese: 而當戶有遺腹子陵，將兵擊胡，兵敗，降匈奴。
sentences.literal: But Danghu had a posthumous son Ling; leading troops he struck the Hu; the army was defeated and he surrendered to the Xiongnu.
sentences.idiomatic: Danghu's posthumous son was Li Ling, who led troops against the Xiongnu, was defeated, and went over to the enemy.

sentences.chinese: 後人告禹謀欲亡從陵，下吏死。
sentences.literal: Afterward someone reported that Yu plotted to flee and join Ling; he was handed to officials and died.
sentences.idiomatic: Later someone accused Li Yu of plotting to defect to Li Ling; he was arrested and died in custody.

sentences.chinese: 陵字少卿，少為侍中建章監。
sentences.literal: Ling's courtesy name was Shaoqing; in youth he was palace attendant and Jianzhang supervisor.
sentences.idiomatic: Li Ling, courtesy name Shaoqing, began his career as palace attendant and supervisor of the Jianzhang Palace.

sentences.chinese: 善騎射，愛人，謙讓下士，甚得名譽。
sentences.literal: Skilled at riding and archery, loving men, yielding and humble toward officers, he obtained great reputation.
sentences.idiomatic: He excelled in horsemanship and the bow, treated others generously, and was modest toward his juniors—qualities that won him wide esteem.

sentences.chinese: 武帝以為有廣之風，使將八百騎，深入匈奴二千餘里，過居延視地形，不見虜，還。
sentences.literal: Emperor Wu thought he had Guang's style, ordered him to lead eight hundred riders, penetrate deeply into the Xiongnu more than two thousand li, pass Juyan to survey terrain, saw no enemy, and returned.
sentences.idiomatic: The emperor saw in him something of Li Guang's dash and sent him with eight hundred horse more than two thousand li into the steppe, beyond Juyan to map the ground. He encountered no Xiongnu and marched home.

sentences.chinese: 拜為騎都尉，將勇敢五千人，教射酒泉、張掖以備胡。
sentences.literal: He was appointed cavalry commandant, leading five thousand picked brave men, teaching archery at Jiuquan and Zhangye to prepare against the Hu.
sentences.idiomatic: He was promoted to cavalry commandant with five thousand picked troops, drilling archery at Jiuquan and Zhangye against future raids.

sentences.chinese: 數年，漢遣貳師將軍伐大宛，使陵將五校兵隨後。
sentences.literal: Several years later the Han dispatched the Ershi general to attack Dayuan, ordering Ling to lead the five colonels' troops following behind.
sentences.idiomatic: Some years afterward, when the Ershi general marched against Dayuan, Li Ling was ordered to follow with the five colonels' contingents.

sentences.chinese: 行至塞，會貳師還。
sentences.literal: When they reached the barrier, it happened that Ershi returned.
sentences.idiomatic: He reached the frontier just as the Ershi column was returning.

sentences.chinese: 上賜陵書，陵留吏士，與輕騎五百出敦煌，至鹽水，迎貳師還，復留屯張掖。
sentences.literal: The emperor bestowed a letter on Ling; Ling retained clerks and soldiers, and with five hundred light riders went out of Dunhuang to the salt-lake region, welcomed Ershi's return, and again remained garrisoned at Zhangye.
sentences.idiomatic: An edict from the emperor reached Li Ling: he was to hold his troops, take five hundred light cavalry from Dunhuang to the saline lakes to meet the returning Ershi force, then go back to his camp at Zhangye.

sentences.chinese: 天漢二年，貳師將三萬騎出酒泉，擊右賢王於天山。
sentences.literal: In the second year of Tianhan the Ershi general led thirty thousand riders out of Jiuquan and struck the Worthy King of the Right at the Celestial Mountains.
sentences.idiomatic: In 99 BCE the Ershi general took thirty thousand cavalry from Jiuquan against the Worthy King of the Right on the Tian Shan range.

sentences.chinese: 召陵，欲使為貳師將輜重。
sentences.literal: He summoned Ling, wishing to make him for Ershi lead the baggage train.
sentences.idiomatic: Li Ling was summoned to escort supplies for the Ershi army.

sentences.chinese: 陵召見武台，叩頭自請曰：「臣所將屯邊者，皆荊楚勇士奇材劍客也，力扼虎，射命中，願得自當一隊，到蘭干山南以分單于兵，毋令專鄉貳師軍。」
sentences.literal: Ling was summoned to audience at the martial terrace, knocked his head and requested of himself, saying, "Those whom your servant leads garrisoning the border are all Jing-Chu bravos, extraordinary talents and wandering swordsmen; their strength strangles tigers, their shooting hits the mark; I wish to obtain to command a separate unit myself, reaching south of Mount Langgan to divide the Chanyu's troops, not letting them concentrate against the Ershi army alone."
sentences.idiomatic: Summoned to the Wu Terrace, Li Ling kowtowed and begged to lead his own detachment: the men he had trained on the frontier were Jing-Chu fighters who could strangle a tiger and never miss with the bow; let him march south of Mount Langgan to pin part of the Chanyu's host so it could not fall entirely on the Ershi column.

sentences.chinese: 上曰：「將惡相屬邪！
sentences.literal: The emperor said, "Do generals dislike being subordinate to one another!
sentences.idiomatic: The emperor snapped, "So generals refuse to serve under one another!

sentences.chinese: 吾發軍多，毋騎予女。」
sentences.literal: I am dispatching many troops; I have no cavalry to give you."
sentences.idiomatic: I have committed every spare mount—there are no cavalry left for you."

sentences.chinese: 陵對：「無所事騎，臣願以少擊眾，步兵五千人涉單于庭。」
sentences.literal: Ling replied, "There is no need to use cavalry; your servant wishes with the few to strike the many—five thousand foot soldiers to tread the Chanyu's court."
sentences.idiomatic: Li Ling answered, "I do not need horse. Give me five thousand foot soldiers and I will march into the heart of the Chanyu's domain."

sentences.chinese: 上壯而許之，因詔強弩都尉路博德將兵半道迎陵軍。
sentences.literal: The emperor thought him stalwart and granted it, thereupon edicting crossbow commandant Lu Bode to lead troops halfway to meet Ling's army.
sentences.idiomatic: Struck by his boldness, the emperor approved and ordered Lu Bode, commandant of strong crossbowmen, to move up the line and cover Li Ling's retreat.

sentences.chinese: 博德故伏波將軍，亦羞為陵後距，奏言：「方秋匈奴馬肥，未可與戰，臣願留陵至春，俱將酒泉、張掖騎各五千人並擊東西浚稽，可必禽也。」
sentences.literal: Bode had formerly been wave-subduing general; he also was ashamed to be Ling's rear guard, and memorialized, saying, "It is just autumn—the Xiongnu horses are fat; one cannot yet fight them; your servant wishes to detain Ling until spring, both leading Jiuquan and Zhangye riders five thousand each jointly to strike eastern and western Junji—then capture can be assured."
sentences.idiomatic: Lu Bode, a former wave-subduing general, was mortified at playing rear guard to Li Ling and memorialized that autumn was the wrong season—the herds were fat; better to hold Li Ling until spring, then strike east and west Junji together with five thousand horse from Jiuquan and five thousand from Zhangye for a sure victory.

sentences.chinese: 書奏，上怒，疑陵悔不欲出而教博德上書，乃詔博德：「吾欲予李陵騎，云『欲以少擊眾』。
sentences.literal: When the memorial was presented the emperor was angry, suspected Ling regretted and did not wish to go out and had taught Bode to memorialize, and thereupon edicted Bode, "I wished to give Li Ling cavalry; he said 'I wish with the few to strike the many.'
sentences.idiomatic: The emperor took the memorial as a conspiracy—Li Ling backing out and putting Lu Bode up to delay—and sent Lu Bode a blistering reply: "I had offered Li Ling cavalry; he insisted on attacking a host with a handful."

sentences.chinese: 今虜入西河，其引兵走西河，遮鉤營之道。」
sentences.literal: Now the enemy have entered Xihe; lead your troops in haste to Xihe and block the road to the Gouying encampment."
sentences.idiomatic: The barbarians are in Xihe—march there at once and seal the approaches to the encampment at Gouying."

sentences.chinese: 詔陵：「以九月發，出庶虜鄣，至東浚稽山南龍勒水上，徘徊觀虜，即亡所見，從浞野侯趙破奴故道抵受降城休士，因騎置以聞。
sentences.literal: He edicted Ling: "Set out in the ninth month, exit the Zhelu barrier fort, reach the southern slopes of Eastern Junji Mountain on the Longle River, linger and observe the enemy; if you see nothing, follow Marquis Zhuoye Zhao Ponu's old route to Accept-Surrender City to rest the troops, then report by mounted courier relay."
sentences.idiomatic: Li Ling was told: "Leave in the ninth month, pass the Zhelu barrier, and take position south of Eastern Junji on the Longle River to scout. If you find no sign of the enemy, follow Zhao Ponu's old track to Accept-Surrender City, rest the column, and send word by relay."

sentences.chinese: 所與博德言者云何？
sentences.literal: What of what you said with Bode?
sentences.idiomatic: What passed between you and Lu Bode?

sentences.chinese: 具以書對。」
sentences.literal: Fully reply in writing."
sentences.idiomatic: Answer every point in a written memorial."

sentences.chinese: 陵於是將其步卒五千人出居延，北行三十日，至浚稽山止營，舉圖所過山川地形，使麾下騎陳步樂還以聞。
sentences.literal: Ling thereupon led his foot soldiers five thousand men out of Juyan, marched north thirty days, reached Junji Mountain and halted camp, raised maps of the mountains and rivers and terrain passed through, and sent his subordinate rider Chen Bule to return and report.
sentences.idiomatic: Li Ling took his five thousand foot out of Juyan, marched north for thirty days, and halted on Junji Mountain, where he charted every ridge and river. He sent his trooper Chen Bule back to court with the maps and a full report.

sentences.chinese: 步樂召見，道陵將率得士死力，上甚說，拜步樂為郎。
sentences.literal: Bule was summoned to audience; he described how Ling as general obtained the officers' death-effort; the emperor was very pleased and appointed Bule gentleman-attendant.
sentences.idiomatic: Chen Bule was brought before the throne and described how Li Ling had inspired his men to fight to the death. The emperor was delighted and made Chen a gentleman-attendant.

sentences.chinese: 陵至浚稽山，與單于相直，騎可三萬圍陵軍。
sentences.literal: Ling reached Junji Mountain, confronted the Chanyu straight on, and about thirty thousand riders surrounded Ling's army.
sentences.idiomatic: On Junji Mountain Li Ling ran head-on into the Chanyu; some thirty thousand horsemen closed a ring around his camp.

sentences.chinese: 軍居兩山間，以大車為營。
sentences.literal: The army dwelt between two mountains, using great carts as camp.
sentences.idiomatic: He drew his wagons into a laager between two ridges.

sentences.chinese: 陵引士出營外為陳，前行持戟盾，後行持弓弩，令曰：「聞鼓聲而縱，聞金聲而止。」
sentences.literal: Ling led the men out beyond the camp to form battle array; the front ranks held halberds and shields, the rear ranks held bows and crossbows; he ordered, "Hear the drum and advance; hear the metal and halt."
sentences.idiomatic: He marched his men outside the wagon ring: spearmen and shieldmen in front, archers behind, with the standing order—advance on the drum, halt at the gong.

sentences.chinese: 虜見漢軍少，直前就營。
sentences.literal: The captives saw the Han army was few and came straight forward toward the camp.
sentences.idiomatic: The Xiongnu, seeing how small the Han detachment was, drove straight for the laager.

sentences.chinese: 陵搏戰攻之，千弩俱發，應弦而倒。
sentences.literal: Ling grapple-fought and attacked them; a thousand crossbows all released together; they responded to the string and fell.
sentences.idiomatic: Li Ling met the charge; a thousand crossbows spoke at once and the enemy wave collapsed.

sentences.chinese: 虜還走上山，漢軍追擊，殺數千人。
sentences.literal: The captives ran back up the mountain; the Han army pursued and struck, killing several thousand men.
sentences.idiomatic: The enemy fled uphill; Li Ling's men pursued and cut down several thousand.

sentences.chinese: 單于大驚，召左右地兵八萬餘騎攻陵。
sentences.literal: The Chanyu was greatly alarmed and summoned left- and right-district troops of more than eighty thousand riders to attack Ling.
sentences.idiomatic: Alarmed, the Chanyu called in more than eighty thousand horse from his left and right wings to crush Li Ling.

sentences.chinese: 陵且戰且引，南行數日，抵山谷中。
sentences.literal: Ling while fighting withdrew, going south several days, reaching a mountain valley.
sentences.idiomatic: Li Ling fought a fighting retreat southward for days until he reached a defile.

sentences.chinese: 連戰，士卒中矢傷，三創者載輦，兩創者將車，一創者持兵戰。
sentences.literal: In successive battles soldiers were hit by arrows and wounded; those with three wounds rode in carts, those with two drove the carts, those with one wound grasped weapons and fought.
sentences.idiomatic: Battle followed battle; men with three wounds were carried in supply carts, those with two drove the wagons, the lightly wounded still stood in the line.

sentences.chinese: 陵曰：「吾士氣少衰而鼓不起者，何也？
sentences.literal: Ling said, "Our officers' spirit is somewhat declining yet the drums do not rise—why is that?
sentences.idiomatic: Li Ling asked, "Our men are flagging, yet the drums will not carry—what is wrong?

sentences.chinese: 軍中豈有女子乎？」
sentences.literal: Could there be women in the army?"
sentences.idiomatic: Are there women hidden in this column?"

sentences.chinese: 始軍出時，關東群盜妻子徙邊者隨軍為卒妻婦，大匿車中。
sentences.literal: When the army first set out, wives of Guandong bandit gangs who had been moved to the border followed the army as soldiers' wives and concubines, largely concealed in the carts.
sentences.idiomatic: At the outset of the march the wives of exiled bandits from east of the passes had stowed away in the baggage train to live as soldiers' women.

sentences.chinese: 陵搜得，皆劍斬之。
sentences.literal: Ling searched them out and all with the sword beheaded them.
sentences.idiomatic: Li Ling had them dragged out and executed every one at sword point.

sentences.chinese: 明日復戰，斬首三千餘級。
sentences.literal: The next day they fought again and took more than three thousand heads.
sentences.idiomatic: The next day's fighting cost the enemy another three thousand dead.

sentences.chinese: 引兵東南，循故龍城道行四五日，抵大澤葭葦中，虜從上風縱火，陵亦令軍中縱火以自救。
sentences.literal: He led the troops southeast, following the old Dragon City road going four or five days, reaching a great marsh amid reeds; the captives from the upwind side set fire, and Ling also ordered the army to set fire to save itself.
sentences.idiomatic: He struck southeast along the old Dragon City track for four or five days into a reed-choked marsh. The Xiongnu set the reeds alight upwind; Li Ling burned a ring of his own to keep the flames off his men.

sentences.chinese: 南行至山下，單于在南山上，使其子將騎擊陵。
sentences.literal: Going south they reached below a mountain; the Chanyu was on the southern mountain and sent his son leading riders to strike Ling.
sentences.idiomatic: Farther south the Chanyu watched from a southern height and sent his son down at the head of cavalry.

sentences.chinese: 陵軍步鬬樹木間，復殺數千人，因發連弩射單于，單于下走。
sentences.literal: Ling's army foot-fought among the trees, again killing several thousand men; thereupon they released repeating crossbows to shoot the Chanyu, and the Chanyu went down and fled.
sentences.idiomatic: Li Ling's infantry fought from tree to tree, killed thousands more, then volleyed repeating crossbows at the Chanyu and drove him from the ridge.

sentences.chinese: 是日捕得虜，言：「單于曰：『此漢精兵，擊之不能下，日夜引吾南近塞，得毋有伏兵乎？』
sentences.literal: That day they captured a prisoner who said, "The Chanyu said, 'These are Han picked troops; striking them we cannot bring them down; day and night they draw us south near the barrier—could there not be ambush troops?'"
sentences.idiomatic: A prisoner taken that day reported the Chanyu's words: "These are Han crack troops—we cannot break them, yet they keep drawing us south toward the passes. Is there an ambush ahead?"

sentences.chinese: 諸當戶君長皆言：『單于自將數萬騎擊漢數千人不能滅，後無以復使邊臣，令漢益輕匈奴。』
sentences.literal: The various household heads and chiefs all said, 'The Chanyu himself leading several tens of thousands of riders cannot destroy several thousand Han—afterward there will be no way again to employ border officials; it will make the Han all the more despise the Xiongnu.'"
sentences.idiomatic: The clan headmen replied: "If the Chanyu in person cannot wipe out a few thousand Han with his main host, he will never again command respect on the frontier—the Han will only despise us more."

sentences.chinese: 復力戰山谷間，尚四五十里得平地，不能破，乃還。」
sentences.literal: Fight again with full force in the mountain valleys; level ground still lies forty or fifty li ahead—if we cannot break them by then, withdraw."
sentences.idiomatic: Keep pressing them in the defiles; the plain is still forty or fifty li off—if we cannot destroy them before that, we should break off and retire."

sentences.chinese: 是時，陵軍益急，匈奴騎多，戰一日數十合，復傷殺虜二千餘人。
sentences.literal: At this time Ling's army was pressed ever more urgently; the Xiongnu riders were many; in one day they fought several tens of engagements, again wounding and killing more than two thousand of the enemy.
sentences.idiomatic: The pressure on Li Ling tightened: the Xiongnu horse swarmed the field, and in dozens of clashes that day his men cut down another two thousand of the enemy.

sentences.chinese: 虜不利，欲去，會陵軍候管敢為校尉所辱，亡降匈奴，具言「陵軍無後救，射矢且盡，獨將軍麾下及成安侯校各八百人為前行，以黃與白為幟，當使精騎射之即破矣。」
sentences.literal: The captives were disadvantaged and wished to withdraw, when it happened that Ling's army scout Guan Gan, having been insulted by a colonel, fled and surrendered to the Xiongnu, reporting in full, "Ling's army has no rear relief; their arrows are nearly exhausted; only the general's personal command and the Marquis of Cheng'an's colonel, eight hundred each as vanguard, use yellow and white as banners—if you send picked riders to shoot them, they will be broken at once."
sentences.idiomatic: The Xiongnu were on the point of quitting when a Han scout named Guan Gan, humiliated by his colonel, defected and betrayed the whole situation: no reserves, arrows almost gone, Li Ling and Han Yannian each leading eight hundred elite in the van under yellow and white pennons—strike that screen with picked bowmen, he said, and the column would shatter.

sentences.chinese: 成安侯者，穎川人，父韓千秋，故濟南相，奮擊南越戰死，武帝封子延年為侯，以校尉隨陵。
sentences.literal: The Marquis of Cheng'an was a man of Yingchuan; his father Han Qianqiu, former chancellor of Jinan, struck hard against Southern Yue and died in battle; Emperor Wu enfeoffed the son Yannian as marquis; as colonel he followed Ling.
sentences.idiomatic: Han Yannian, Marquis of Cheng'an, came from Yingchuan; his father Han Qianqiu had died fighting the Southern Yue, and the son had inherited the marquisate. He now served Li Ling as a colonel.

sentences.chinese: 單于得敢大喜，使騎並攻漢軍，疾呼曰：「李陵、韓延年趣降！」
sentences.literal: The Chanyu obtained Gan and was greatly pleased; he ordered riders jointly to attack the Han army, shouting urgently, "Li Ling, Han Yannian—hasten to surrender!"
sentences.idiomatic: The Chanyu was jubilant. He threw every rider forward in a massed assault, shouting for Li Ling and Han Yannian to yield at once.

sentences.chinese: 遂遮道急攻陵。
sentences.literal: Thereupon they blocked the road and urgently attacked Ling.
sentences.idiomatic: They sealed the defile and threw wave after wave at Li Ling.

sentences.chinese: 陵居谷中，虜在山上，四面射，矢如雨下。
sentences.literal: Ling dwelt in the valley; the captives were on the mountain; from four sides they shot; arrows fell like rain.
sentences.idiomatic: Li Ling was trapped in the bottom of a gorge while the Xiongnu ringed the heights and poured arrows into the bowl.

sentences.chinese: 漢軍南行，未至鞮汗山，一日五十萬矢皆盡，即棄車去。
sentences.literal: The Han army went south; before reaching Di-han Mountain, in one day five hundred thousand arrows were all exhausted; then they abandoned the carts and departed.
sentences.idiomatic: They fought southward toward Di-han Mountain until half a million shafts were gone in a single day; then they abandoned the wagons.

sentences.chinese: 士尚三千餘人，徒斬車輻而持之，軍吏持尺刀，抵山入陿谷。
sentences.literal: The soldiers still were more than three thousand; they merely chopped cart spokes and grasped them; army clerks grasped foot-long knives; they reached the mountain and entered a narrow valley.
sentences.idiomatic: Barely three thousand men remained, armed with hacked-off wheel spokes while the officers clutched short swords; they clawed their way toward a narrow pass.

sentences.chinese: 單于遮其後，乘隅下壘石，士卒多死，不得行。
sentences.literal: The Chanyu blocked their rear; taking advantage of a corner they rolled down piled stones; many soldiers died; they could not advance.
sentences.idiomatic: The Chanyu closed the rear of the column and rolled boulders from the cliffs; the dead piled up and the Han could not move.

sentences.chinese: 昏後，陵便衣獨步出營，止左右：「毋隨我，丈夫一取單于耳！」
sentences.literal: After dusk Ling in plain clothes alone walked out of the camp, stopping those at his sides, "Do not follow me—a man takes the Chanyu's ear in one go!"
sentences.idiomatic: After dark Li Ling changed into civilian dress, slipped out of camp alone, and waved his men back: "Stay where you are—I mean to bring back the Chanyu's head myself."

sentences.chinese: 良久，陵還，大息曰：「兵敗，死矣！」
sentences.literal: After a long while Ling returned, greatly sighing, saying, "The army is defeated—we are dead!"
sentences.idiomatic: He came back a long while later, breathing hard: "The fight is lost—we are finished."

sentences.chinese: 軍吏或曰：「將軍威震匈奴，天命不遂，後求道徑還歸，如浞野侯為虜所得，後亡還，天子客遇之，況於將軍乎！」
sentences.literal: An army clerk said, "The general's might shakes the Xiongnu; Heaven's mandate did not succeed; afterward seek a path and return—as when the Zhuoye Marquis was taken by the captives and afterward fled back, the Son of Heaven treated him as a guest—how much more the general!"
sentences.idiomatic: One of his officers urged him on: "Your name still terrifies the Xiongnu. Heaven has simply turned its face; find a track home as Zhao Ponu did after capture—the emperor received him courteously. How much more would he welcome you?"

sentences.chinese: 陵曰：「公止！
sentences.literal: Ling said, "Sir, stop!
sentences.idiomatic: Li Ling cut him short:

sentences.chinese: 吾不死，非壯士也。」
sentences.literal: If I do not die, I am not a stalwart man."
sentences.idiomatic: A man who will not die now is no soldier at all."

sentences.chinese: 於是盡斬旌旗，及珍寶埋地中，陵歎曰：「復得數十矢，足以脫矣。
sentences.literal: Thereupon they utterly cut down the banners and flags, and precious things they buried in the ground; Ling sighed, saying, "If again we had several tens of arrows, it would suffice to escape.
sentences.idiomatic: They hacked down the standards, buried what treasure they had, and Li Ling groaned, "Another sheaf of arrows and we might still break out.

sentences.chinese: 今無兵復戰，天明坐受縛矣！
sentences.literal: Now without arms to fight again, at daybreak we will sit and be bound!
sentences.idiomatic: We have no weapons left—at dawn we sit here to be tied like sheep!

sentences.chinese: 各鳥獸散，猶有得脫歸報天子者。」
sentences.literal: Each scatter like birds and beasts—still there may be those who escape and return to report to the Son of Heaven."
sentences.idiomatic: Scatter like birds and beasts—some of you may yet reach the emperor with word of what happened."

sentences.chinese: 令軍士人持二升糒，一半冰，期至遮虜鄣者相待。
sentences.literal: He ordered the soldiers each to hold two sheng of dried rations and half a block of ice, appointing that those who reached the Zhelu barrier should wait for one another.
sentences.idiomatic: He issued each man two measures of dry rations and a lump of ice and told them to rally again at the Zhelu barrier if they could win through.

sentences.chinese: 夜半時，擊鼓起士，鼓不鳴。
sentences.literal: In the middle of the night they beat the drum to rouse the troops; the drum did not sound.
sentences.idiomatic: At midnight he ordered the general alarm; the drum gave no answering roll.

sentences.chinese: 陵與韓延年俱上馬，壯士從者十餘人。
sentences.literal: Ling and Han Yannian together mounted horses; stalwart followers were more than ten men.
sentences.idiomatic: Li Ling and Han Yannian vaulted into the saddle; a dozen picked men rode with them.
